does: This paper develops multilingual sparse autoencoders (SAEs) for reliable language steering in large language models. The authors train SAEs on multilingual data rather than English-only data and show this consistently improves cross-lingual feature representations and steering quality. They introduce a principled layer-selection rule based on the intersection of multilingual alignment and language separability, enabling prediction of effective intervention depths without exhaustive search. The approach is evaluated on LLaMA-3.1-8B and Gemma-2-9B across machine translation and cross-lingual summarization tasks, demonstrating that multilingual SAEs with intersection-selected steering layers achieve more reliable language control while preserving output quality compared to heuristic baselines.
connects: This work builds on the emerging literature using sparse autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability and activation steering in LLMs, extending these techniques from monolingual to multilingual settings. It responds to the limitation that existing SAE-based steering methods rely on English-only training data and heuristic layer selection, which fails in cross-lingual contexts. The paper connects to prior work on understanding multilingual representations in neural networks, language separability metrics, and principled intervention strategies in neural models. It also engages with recent advances in SAE training methodology and their application to model control, adapting these frameworks to handle the complexities of multilingual language modeling where different languages must be simultaneously represented and steerable.
enables: This work enables more reliable deployment of language control mechanisms in multilingual AI systems, opening paths toward better-controlled machine translation, cross-lingual content generation, and language-specific model customization. The principled layer-selection framework could extend beyond language steering to other domains requiring intervention in multilingual models, such as bias reduction or cultural alignment across languages. The methodology establishes foundations for developing multilingual sparse autoencoders as standard interpretability tools, potentially facilitating deeper mechanistic understanding of how multilingual LLMs organize linguistic knowledge. Future work could apply these techniques to more languages, investigate steering trade-offs across language pairs, and develop refined metrics for predicting steering efficacy in zero-shot cross-lingual scenarios.
